name: Process and Analyze Log Files with awk/sed/grep
description: Build a log processing pipeline to extract, filter, aggregate, and summarize information from application and server logs.
trendingReason: CLI log processing is 10-100x faster than loading logs into a GUI tool for quick analysis

steps.title: Filter log lines by level or pattern
steps.description: Use grep to extract only error or warning lines from a log file.
steps.tip: Use grep -i for case-insensitive matching or grep -c to count matches.

steps.title: Extract specific fields with awk
steps.description: Parse structured log lines to extract timestamps, status codes, or response times.
steps.tip: $1 is the first field, $NF is the last field. Change the delimiter with -F: awk -F',' for CSV logs.

steps.title: Count occurrences and find top errors
steps.description: Aggregate log entries to find the most frequent errors or status codes.
steps.command: grep 'ERROR' /var/log/app.log | awk '{print $NF}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

steps.title: Extract log entries in a time range
steps.description: Filter logs between two timestamps using awk comparison.

steps.title: Generate a summary report
steps.description: Create a one-liner that produces a complete log summary with counts by level.
steps.command:
awk '/ERROR/{e++} /WARN/{w++} /INFO/{i++} END{printf "Errors: %d
Warnings: %d
Info: %d
Total: %d
", e, w, i, NR}' /var/log/app.log

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

LOG_FILE=${1:?'Usage: $0 <logfile>'}

[[ ! -f "$LOG_FILE" ]] && { echo "File not found: $LOG_FILE" >&2; exit 1; }

echo "=== Log Summary: $LOG_FILE ==="
echo "Total lines: $(wc -l < "$LOG_FILE")"
echo ""

echo "=== Entries by Level ==="
awk '/ERROR/{e++} /WARN/{w++} /INFO/{i++} /DEBUG/{d++}
  END{
    printf "  ERROR:  %d\n  WARN:   %d\n  INFO:   %d\n  DEBUG:  %d\n", e+0, w+0, i+0, d+0
  }' "$LOG_FILE"

echo ""
echo "=== Top 10 Errors ==="
grep 'ERROR' "$LOG_FILE" | sed 's/.*ERROR//' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

echo ""
echo "=== Errors per Hour ==="
grep 'ERROR' "$LOG_FILE" | awk '{print substr($2,1,13)}' | sort | uniq -c

echo ""
echo "=== Last 5 Errors ==="
grep 'ERROR' "$LOG_FILE" | tail -5

faq.question: When should I use grep vs awk vs sed?
faq.answer: Use grep for finding lines that match a pattern. Use awk for extracting and processing fields from structured text. Use sed for transforming or replacing text in-place. For complex processing, pipe them together: grep to filter, awk to extract, sort/uniq to aggregate.

faq.question: How do I process compressed log files?
faq.answer: Use zgrep, zcat, and zless for .gz files. For example: zgrep 'ERROR' /var/log/app.log.gz | awk '{print $NF}' | sort | uniq -c. This avoids decompressing the entire file.

faq.question: How do I handle multi-line log entries like stack traces?
faq.answer: Use awk with a record separator: awk 'BEGIN{RS="\n[0-9]{4}-"} /ERROR/' to treat each log entry as a record. Or use grep -A 10 'ERROR' to show 10 lines after each match.
